headline: English rendering of PM’s address at the launch of development works in Surat, Gujarat
fullText:
English rendering of PM’s address at the launch of development works in Surat, Gujarat

Bharat Mata ki Jai. Bharat Mata ki Jai. Bharat Mata ki Jai.

Popular Chief Minister of Gujarat Shri Bhupendra Bhai Patel, my colleague in the Union Cabinet C. R. Patil, Deputy Chief Minister Bhai Harsh Sanghvi, Gujarat BJP President Jagdish Vishwakarma ji, other ministers present, public representatives, and my dear brothers and sisters of Surat.

How are you all? And Surat is enjoying itself, isn’t it? What have you brought, brother? Thank you, friend, you have made a very beautiful picture. SPG people, this brother wants to present it, thank you very much, brother, please give it to my team.

Before I begin my words, just a few days ago my close companion, with whom I worked for many years and who remained active until the last moments of his life, Kanubhai Mavani, departed. I respectfully pay tribute to him. The pain of losing a companion is natural, but today as I stand on the soil of Surat, I continue my words with his sacred remembrance.

Many times I feel that Surat is not just a city, Surat is a spirit. And when this spirit exists, then on Environment Day, every family joins together, and such 15,000 environment-dedicated posters are created and brought here. I heartily congratulate all those families who did this work. Because when they thought of this, their minds became engaged in protecting the environment. And many of our Kathiawad brothers live here in Surat, and they have farms there. I request them to join the “Save the Farm” campaign as part of environmental work. Chemical-free farming, the natural farming our elders practiced. And I say with full right to all the people of Surat who have farms, wherever they are, that this is the work I ask of you.

Everyone here welcomed me, gave me different souvenirs, gifts. But apart from that, Surat has given me an invaluable gift, even more precious. Surat has given me garbage as a gift. One lakh people carried out a cleaning campaign for five days. For me, this is the greatest gift, and I thank one lakh people and the entire Surat from my heart. People may wonder, what kind of Prime Minister is this? He is proud of receiving garbage as a welcome gift. Because for me, cleanliness must become a value. One lakh people joining means one lakh families joining. And this is not just a cleanliness campaign, it is a kind of festival of values for a healthy future. For this, all the companions of Surat deserve great congratulations.

After the local body elections, I have come to Gujarat for the first time, to Surat. From Surat, I bow and congratulate the people of Gujarat.

It has been more than two and a half decades that you have continuously given your blessings to us, to the BJP. And with time, these blessings have only increased. See, one daughter pulled you all along. Recently, elections were held for district councils, taluka councils, municipalities, and municipal corporations. In these, the people of Gujarat gave so much support to the BJP that all old records were broken. And today I am even happier because you broke my records too. I came into politics very late, perhaps in 1987, and then the first election was of Surat–Ahmedabad Corporation, and in that first election we achieved a massive victory. And since then, this journey of victory has continued. In the democratic societies of the world, such a thing is rarely seen. For any party to receive the blessings of the people for such a long time and the opportunity to serve, this is a very great thing.

Many elected representatives of Surat and Navsari are also present here. I extend my best wishes to all of you. We must always remember, the people of Gujarat have supported our spirit of service. This massive victory is to further expand this mission of service. We must all work even harder. Our resolve is a developed Gujarat, a developed India. This resolve must be fulfilled with dedication to service. When I say Surat is not a city, Surat is a spirit, behind it is my conviction. Imagine, in the whole of India, a city that continuously receives awards for cleanliness. Naturally, one might think, enough, we have received so many awards, what more is there to do? But Surat does not think like that. It still prioritizes cleanliness campaigns. That is why I say Surat is not a city, Surat is a spirit.

Our resolve of a developed India, a developed Gujarat-this resolve will be fulfilled only when we develop every village, every district, every city of the country. Therefore, the role of all elected BJP representatives is very important.

Today is also a pleasant coincidence. Today is June 5, Environment Day. And on this Environment Day, I am in Surat, one of the cleanest cities in the country. And how great a matter of pride this is! This is the same Surat that was once affected by a plague-like epidemic, and today it is recognized for cleanliness. For this, continuous efforts have been made for two and a half decades. All the people of Surat, officers, employees, public representatives-everyone has contributed. I have been told that in recent days, special campaigns for cleanliness have also been carried out here. I congratulate all the residents of Surat, my dear Suratis, very much. To accelerate the development of Surat, many projects have been inaugurated and foundation stones laid here today. I extend my heartfelt congratulations to all of you for these projects.

The world is now very cautious about moving towards a green era, a Green Future, and is trying to take steps gradually. India too is working extensively on green growth. And Gujarat had already taken fast steps in this direction many years ago. Gujarat is the state whose government created a separate department for climate change at the beginning of this century. Gujarat was the first state in the country to create such an arrangement. And as the Chief Minister just mentioned, in Gujarat’s own Charanka village in Patan, India’s first solar park was also built. And at that time, it became a kind of pilgrimage site. People came to see such a huge solar plant visible far and wide.

What you all did in Gujarat at that time inspired the whole country. Today India is developing with the mantra of progress with nature. Economy as well as ecology.

This is clearly visible in Surat. Nowadays, Surat’s “circular water economy” is much discussed. Here, the city’s wastewater is treated and supplied to industry. This manages dirty water and also earns from water. For the past 12 years, a major mass movement of waste to wealth has been going on in the country. This is greatly helping in the cleanliness of our cities and in making them green and clean.

Now the effort is to ensure the complete arrangement of drinking water for Surat for many decades to come. For this, the Tapi Barrage Project has been approved. In addition, water management and water drainage are also very big priorities of the government. Many such projects have been inaugurated here today.

Surat is not limited only to cleanliness and a circular economy. The expansion of other means of green growth is also happening at unprecedented speed. For example, electric mobility. Surat is working to create a system where all buses running on the roads will be electric. Along with this, the expansion of Surat Metro is also happening. All these steps will further strengthen Surat’s identity as a green city. And yes, in the coming time, Hazira Industrial Area will also be known for the production of green steel. That is, green energy will be used in steel production.

Today the world is passing through a period of unprecedented challenges. Some time ago I had said that this decade is proving to be a decade of disasters for the world. We have seen one global disaster after another in recent times. First came the huge crisis of Corona, then wars broke out in different places, and now such a massive energy crisis has thrown the entire world into turmoil. Petrol prices are fluctuating continuously across the world, and gas supply chains are collapsing.

I feel great satisfaction that with the collective efforts of 1.4 billion Indians, the country is strongly facing every such crisis. You are with me in this fight, aren’t you? We have to win this fight too, don’t we?

In this too, Gujarat has a very big role. In recent years, Gujarat has strengthened its capacity in refining, solar, and wind power, which is proving very useful for the country. Today India’s renewable capacity is 250 gigawatts. In our country, earlier we never thought beyond megawatts, today we are talking about gigawatts. And Gujarat alone contributes 50 gigawatts. Out of 250 gigawatts, 50 gigawatts is your strength. That means one-fifth of the country’s green energy is produced by Gujarat alone. Gujarat has done commendable work in solar energy production. And now, when the country is working in areas like green hydrogen and green ammonia, Gujarat’s role will also be very significant.

This ongoing global crisis shows how important self-reliance is in the matter of energy. And the capacity the country has built in the past 12 years is of immense importance. On one hand, we ensured the supply of oil and gas from different parts of the world. On the other hand, we made historic investments in renewable energy. Twelve years ago, solar energy production in India was negligible. Today we are among the top five countries in the world. And not just solar energy-we increased ethanol blending capacity, accelerated railway electrification, worked on nuclear energy, modernized and expanded our old electricity transmission network, greatly expanded the gas pipeline network, and increased the capacity of our ports so that more petroleum products could reach there and be stored where possible. In the coming time, the country’s capacity will increase even more. Our government’s efforts in this direction are continuously ongoing.

Before coming among you here in Surat, I went to Hazira, which Harsh Bhai was just describing. What self-reliant India means is clearly visible in Hazira. Hazira today is not just an industrial area. It has become an ecosystem where there is energy, steel, defense production, ports, and global trade. Hazira is developing as a major maritime-industrial hub of the country, emerging as a major center of self-reliant India.

Today there are some pessimistic people in the country who keep mocking the self-reliant India campaign. They constantly belittle this resolve of the nation. These are the people who have always kept India dependent on other countries. They forget that a country dependent on others can never achieve the heights of development it deserves.

To accelerate self-reliant India, today the country is focusing greatly on connectivity. Projects like the Vadodara–Mumbai Expressway, Dedicated Freight Corridor, and Bullet Train reflect this priority of India. Today a major section of the Vadodara–Mumbai Expressway has been inaugurated. This will provide better connectivity between two major industrial and trade centers of the country.

Our effort is to connect everyone, to give equal opportunities and facilities to all. Remote areas, tribal regions, which never had access to facilities, are today getting modern connectivity. The Dahod–Bodeli–Vapi corridor reflects this resolve. Imagine, four-lane facilities are reaching the tribal areas of Narmada and Tapi districts. This will reduce travel time, reduce fares, and give tribal and rural people excellent facilities for education, healthcare, and livelihood.

Through this corridor, the tribal areas of Chhota Udepur, Narmada, Bharuch, and Tapi districts will be connected to the industrial belt. This will also boost tourism in the tribal areas of South Gujarat. Access to the Statue of Unity, Saputara, and nearby tourist destinations will become much easier.

Today here in Surat, a modern ESIC hospital has also been inaugurated. This hospital will become a new center of better healthcare services for our worker brothers and sisters and their families. Companions who have come here from other states to live and work will also benefit greatly from this hospital.

Our government is working with national development as the top priority. That is why the country’s trust is in the BJP, in the BJP’s development work. That is why the people of the country are repeatedly giving the BJP a mandate. Recently, elections in Bengal-you may feel energy rise as soon as I mention Bengal-but my experience was that during my recent visit to five countries, Bengal was being discussed everywhere. Everyone talks about Bengal. In the elections of Bengal, Assam, and Puducherry, the BJP, NDA received a huge mandate. Apart from this, in the Panchayat and Corporation elections happening across the country, BJP is also winning.

Every such election carries one message: the country does not like chaos, uncertainty, and despair. Congress has been spreading chaos and uncertainty for the past 12 years to find opportunities for itself, but the people of the country are repeatedly giving it a strong reply. You people of Gujarat have pushed Congress to the margins, but even where Congress has governments, the people are fed up with Congress’s misrule. Recently, local body elections were held in Himachal, where Congress is in power, and Congress was badly defeated. The people of Himachal rejected Congress’s misrule. Before that, Congress lost in Haryana’s local body elections, and the people of Punjab also gave Congress a clear message. The message is that Congress’s parasitic politics will not work, Congress’s politics of finding opportunities in chaos will not work. Even in Karnataka, there is great anger among the people against the Congress government. And that is why Congress is having to change the Chief Minister in Karnataka too.

India has moved far beyond negativity. This is a country of unlimited optimism. A country full of extraordinary aspirations. The citizens of India are filled with dreams, filled with resolve. And the people are committed to turning resolve into accomplishment. And when the people of the country are determined, the country can achieve every goal. And this, this is India’s strength. Let us all together accelerate the building of a developed India. Let us turn dreams into resolve, and resolve into accomplishment by dedicating ourselves fully. I stand shoulder to shoulder with you. If you walk two steps, I am ready to walk three. Stopping and tiring is not acceptable to us. With this call, once again, many congratulations for the development projects.

Disclaimer: Some portions of the Prime Minister’s speech were in Gujarati, which have been translated here in Hindi essence.

summaryText:
**1. CONTEXT**  
The speech was delivered by Prime Minister Narendra Modi at the launch of a series of development projects in Surat, Gujarat, on 5 June 2024, a date chosen deliberately for its dual symbolism – *International Environment Day* and the state‑wide celebration of the city’s extraordinary record in the Swachh Bharat Mission. The PM’s address came as part of a larger strategy: to reinforce the narrative of “cleanliness as a value” and to showcase the federal partnership with state governments in achieving sustainable urban development. Modi, a home‑grown politician from the Western state, flanked by senior BJP leaders from Gujarat, used the occasion to applaud local participation, to memorialize a late colleague, and to set an ideological tone for the Green Growth agenda that the Central Government is championing through its National Green Initiative (Greening India Policy 2025).  

The address also followed a recent wave of elected bodies across the state – district councils, municipal corporations, and taluka bodies – which, according to the PM, “explain the unprecedented vote‐share” that the BJP has kept receiving over the last twenty‑five years. It is a moment where political, environmental and developmental narratives converge, and hence an excellent case study for students preparing for the UPSC General Studies Paper‑III (India’s Economy, Society and Governance).

---

**2. KEY FEATURES**  
| Feature | Explanation | Significance for UPSC |
|---------|-------------|-----------------------|
| **Use of Emotional Invocation** | Modi opens with a heartfelt tribute to the late Kanubhai Mavani, a local politician, and continues with personal anecdotes (e.g., “Sanitization as a gift”). | Demonstrates the “connective discourse” that dominates modern Indian politics, useful for analysis of political communication (PS). |
| **Glocal Analogy **| Surat is portrayed as a “spirit” rather than a “city,” emphasising local culture and citizen activism. | Examines the concept of “glocalism,” a recurring theme in Indian nation‑state dynamics (PS). |
| **Statistical Appeals** | He cites “15,000 posters” for Environment Day, “one lakh people” in a cleanliness drive, and “hundreds of records” broken in local elections. | Shows the PM’s reliance on numerical glamour – a feature in the debates on data‑driven governance (GS I). |
| **Environmental Diplomacy** | Draws a parallel between India’s national green agenda and Gujarat’s pioneering climate change department. | Illustrates theory–practice gaps and cases of inter‑governmental cooperation (GS II, IIc). |
| **Political Narrative** | He frames the BJP’s success as a “continuous blessing” – inviting an ideological reading of electoral politics in Gujarat. | Offers material for the ideology of the political parties & nationality debates (PS). |
| **Infrastructure Announcements** | Mentions foundation stones laid and development works inaugurated. | Illustrates the “glitz” of inaugurations in contemporary politics – a point of discussion in the integuments of development (GS I). |
| **Public Participation** | Calls for citizen activism in “Save the Farm” campaigns and encourages chemical‑free farming. | Contextualises “participatory governance” and agricultural sustainability (GS III). |

---

**3. SIGNIFICANCE**  
* **Micro‑Meso‑Macro Linkages** – The speech is a microcosm of the broader Interaction between the centre and the state. Gujarat is shown to be a model for “cleanliness” and “green development” and is fashioned as a stepping stone in India’s national Green Growth agenda.  
* **Narrativisation of Cleanliness** – Reflexively turning sanitation into a cultural value vaulted the Swachh Bharat Mission from a mere flagship programme to a social movement. The speech’s emphasis on “garbage as a gift” frames the act of cleaning as a virtuous expression of civic responsibility.  
* **Ideological Engine** – The emphasis on “service” and “development” is a repeated motif in BJP’s oratory, which provides rich material for understanding the party’s political branding in the context of Hindutva and developmental paradigm.  
* **Environmental Governance Layer** – Gujarat’s early climate change policy is presented as a “cure” for ecological deterioration. The narrative draws on the theoretical concept of stewardship, thereby offering a concrete case for policy‑analysis debates in the UPSC syllabus.  
* **Citizen‑State Interaction** – The induction of local bodies and formation of a “cleanliness culture” illustrates India’s approach to “participatory governance.” Such audits become real examples in the study of federalism, local self‑government (Panchayati Raj) and decentralised planning.

---

**4. UPSC RELEVANCE**  
| Syllabus Topic | How the Speech Helps | Examination Aspect |
|---|---|---|
| *Development Strategy (I* | Demonstrates how central campaigns (Swachh Bharat, Green Growth) are married with state‑level initiatives (Gujarat’s climate department). | Requires *policy analysis* – evaluate continuity, and integration of central‑state policies. |
| *Political & Ideological Thought (PS* | Provides material for the ideological underpinnings of the BJP, use of emotional discourse, narrative construction, and the role of local leaders (Kanubhai Mavani etc.). | Exams need *critique of political ideology* and *role of oratory* in shaping public opinion. |
| *Climate Change and Environmental Studies* | Showcases tangible actions: the climate department, cleanliness drives, green infrastructure, promotion of chemical‑free farming. | Tests *knowledge of global climate initiatives, national policies*, and *the role of local implementation*. |
| *Governance – Local & State Government* | Focuses on the importance of local bodies in Gujarat (district, taluka, municipal elections). | Evaluation of *decentralised governance*, *autonomy*, and *political dynamics at the local level*. |
| *Institutional Inter‑governmental Relations* | Central–state partnership highlighted in the launch of development works. | Requirement for answering questions on *federal structure*, *inter‑governmental relations*, *policy implementation mechanisms*. |
| *Sustainable Development* | Embeds the idea of green growth, clean waste, and sustainable agriculture. | Helps answer questions on *sustainable development index* and *Indo‑Pak* broader environmental policies. |
| *Communication and Public Policy (PS & GS* | Measures how speech uses emotional appeal, contextual data, and rhetorical devices. | Provides case for *public policy communication* studies – analysis of the “instrumental use of language”. |

**Suggested MCQs**  
1. Which environmental service is most directly referenced in the PM’s speech?  
   a) Green Roads Program; b) Swachh Bharat Mission; c) National Clean Air Program; d) Surface Water Management Project.  
2. Modi’s description of Surat as a “spirit” primarily exemplifies which political communication strategy?  
   a) Pragmatic policy; b) Symbolic governance; c) Incremental development; d) Communicative rationalization.  

**Essay question (Paper‑III)**: *Analyse the role of political rhetoric in the implementation of the Swachh Bharat Mission using the PM’s Speech in Surat as a primary source.*

---

### Concluding Notes  
The PM’s address offers a multi‑layered case study for UPSC candidates: from the deployment of narrative tools to highlight policy successes, to concrete action on environmental sustainability and local governance. For an aspirant, dissecting this speech gives an understanding not just of India’s development trajectory, but also of how political leaders translate abstract policy into emotional, mobilising narratives that resonate with the electorate. The challenge for the candidate is to contextualise this speech within the larger framework of Central‑State relations, global climate obligations and the emerging ethos of “cleanliness as citizenship.”

headline: Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi inaugurates, dedicates to the nation and lays the foundation stone of multiple development projects worth over ₹18,800 crore in Surat, Gujarat
fullText:
Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi inaugurates, dedicates to the nation and lays the foundation stone of multiple development projects worth over ₹18,800 crore in Surat, Gujarat A series of transformative projects in the road, power and industrial sectors are being launched from Surat today; These initiatives will drive growth and improve ‘Ease of Living’ for the people: PMToday, India is advancing its development guided by the principle of 'progress in harmony with nature': PMA massive 'waste to wealth' public movement has been active in the country for the past 12 years; This is significantly contributing to city sanitation and helping keep our urban areas green and clean: PMToday, the world is facing an unprecedented set of challenges, But with the collective efforts of 140 crore Indians, the country continues to move ahead with confidence and optimism: PM

The Prime Minister, Shri Narendra Modi, today inaugurated and laid foundation stones for several development projects in Surat, Gujarat. Addressing a large gathering, the Prime Minister expressed his gratitude to the people of Gujarat for their unwavering support. "After the local body elections, I am visiting Surat for the first time. From Surat, I salute and congratulate the people of the entire Gujarat," remarked Shri Modi.

Extending congratulations to the many elected representatives from Surat and Navsari present at the event, the Prime Minister reminded all stakeholders that the people of Gujarat have endorsed the spirit of service. Emphasizing that this massive victory is a mandate to further expand the mission of service, he called upon all elected representatives to work even harder. He underscored the significant role that all elected public representatives must play in realizing this vision. "Our resolve is to build a Viksit Gujarat and Viksit Bharat. This resolve will be fulfilled only by developing every village, every district, every city of the country," asserted Shri Modi.

Drawing attention to the auspicious coincidence of the event taking place on Environment Day, the Prime Minister expressed pride in being in Surat, one of the cleanest cities of the country, on June 5. Recalling Surat's transformation from a city once affected by the plague epidemic to being recognized today for its cleanliness, he lauded the continuous efforts made over the past two and a half decades by all stakeholders, including citizens, officers, employees, and public representatives. "What a matter of great pride it is, this very Surat, which was once affected by the plague epidemic, is today being recognized for cleanliness," remarked Shri Modi.

Tracing Gujarat’s pioneering role in the global transition toward a green future, the Prime Minister recalled the state's foresight in establishing India's first Climate Change Department in 2009 alongside the historic Charanka Solar Park. He detailed how Surat perfectly exemplifies the national mantra of balancing progress with nature through its innovative 'Circular Water Economy', treating wastewater for industrial use, and the nationwide 'waste to wealth' movement of the past twelve years. Ensuring future drinking water and drainage security, he highlighted the approval of the Tapi Barrage Project, while also anticipating Surat's rapid expansion into electric mobility, the growing metro network, and the Hazira Industrial Area's upcoming green steel production powered entirely by renewable energy. '“All these steps, from ensuring our buses are electric to producing steel using green energy, will further empower Surat's distinct identity as a truly green city,”' noted Shri Modi.

Addressing the unprecedented challenges of what he termed a 'decade of disasters', spanning the pandemic, global conflicts, and volatile energy supply chains, the Prime Minister praised the 140 crore Indians for firmly withstanding these massive global shocks. He specifically credited Gujarat for its heavy contribution to India’s energy self-reliance, noting the state produces one-fifth (50 GW) of the nation's 250 GW renewable energy capacity and is poised to lead in emerging sectors like Green Hydrogen and Green Ammonia. Underscoring the critical importance of the energy capabilities built over the last twelve years, he outlined historic investments in solar power, ethanol blending, railway electrification, nuclear energy, modernized electricity transmission networks, and the massive expansion of gas pipelines and port storage capacities. '“This ongoing global crisis shows how absolutely necessary self-reliance is in terms of energy, and the vast capacity the country has built over the last twelve years holds immense importance today,”' observed Shri Modi.

Reflecting on his visit to Hazira prior to the event, the Prime Minister described the region not merely as an industrial zone, but as a comprehensive, thriving ecosystem encompassing energy, steel, defense production, and global maritime trade. He positioned this Maritime-Industrial Hub as a living testament to the 'Aatmanirbhar Bharat' (Self-Reliant India) initiative, strongly condemning pessimistic factions within the country who mock the self-reliance campaign and who historically kept the nation dependent on foreign entities. '“Those people who continuously demean the nation's resolve forget that a country dependent on others can never achieve the great heights of development which it rightfully deserves,”' emphasized Shri Modi.

Outlining the government's intense focus on modern connectivity to drive self-reliance, the Prime Minister cited mega-projects like the Dedicated Freight Corridor, the Bullet Train, and the newly inaugurated section of the Vadodara-Mumbai Expressway as vital catalysts for industrial and trade growth. He emphasized that this infrastructure drive is inherently inclusive, reaching previously underserved tribal and remote regions through initiatives like the Dahod-Bodeli-Vapi corridor. By bringing four-lane highways to the tribal belts of Chhota Udepur, Narmada, Bharuch, and Tapi, the government is reducing travel time and freight costs while simultaneously unlocking unprecedented access to education, healthcare, and tourism at destinations like the Statue of Unity and Saputara. '“By bringing modern connectivity to our tribal and rural areas, we are ensuring they receive excellent facilities for education, medicine, and enhanced earning opportunities,”' affirmed Shri Modi.

Inaugurating a modern ESIC hospital in Surat to provide better healthcare for workers and migrant families from other states, the Prime Minister connected these developmental milestones to the overwhelming electoral trust the nation continues to place in the government. Concluding with a resounding call to action, he celebrated the limitless optimism of the Indian citizens, expressing absolute confidence in the collective power of the people to build a fully developed nation. '“India is a country equipped with incredible aspirations and limitless optimism, and when the manpower of the nation is completely resolved, there is no goal we cannot achieve,” said Shri Modi.

**1. CONTEXT**  
Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi, on 5 June 2024 – Environment Day – inaugurated a portfolio of development projects in Surat, Gujarat, with a cumulative valuation of ₹18,800 crore.  The spectrum spans road‑infrastructure, power generation, industrial hubs and public‑health facilities.  The PM’s remarks foreground Gujarat’s legacy of green‑innovation (e.g., first Climate Change Department, `Circular Water Economy`) and its role as a bellwether for India’s “Self‑Re‑Liance” (Aatmanirbhar Bharat) agenda.  Surrounded by a large gathering of elected representatives, citizens and dignitaries, the address was aimed at reinforcing the partnership between central and local governments.  

**2. KEY FEATURES**  
| Domain | Project Highlights | Strategic Significance |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Road & Connectivity** | 4‑lane highways to tribal belts (Chhota Udepur, Narmada, Bharuch, Tapi), Vadodara‑Mumbai Expressway (new segment), Dedicated Freight Corridor, Bullet Train (concept). | Cuts freight cost, boosts regional trade, improves access to education & health. |
| **Power & Energy** | Tapi Barrage for irrigation, green‑steel production at Hazira, additional solar & wind capacity (50 GW of Gujarat’s 250 GW total). | Reinforces energy self‑reliance, supports green‑hydrogen & ammonia projects. |
| **Industrial & Economic** | Hazira Industrial Complex expansion, eco‑friendly steel sector, maritime‑industrial hub. | Sets industry–maritime nexus, reduces import dependence, enhances export corridors. |
| **Urban & Public Health** | Modern ESIC hospital in Surat (workers  & migrant families); waste‑to‑wealth movement. | Improves occupational health, reduces urban congestion, showcases sanitation success. |
| **Environment & Sustainability** | Circular water economy, climate‑change initiatives, clean‑city status. | Demonstrates integrating development with nature, models low‑carbon growth. |

**3. SIGNIFICANCE**  
The Surat blueprint epitomises a multi‑layered growth model:  
* **Integrated infrastructure**—transport, energy and industrial zones linked through high‑tech corridors, enabling logistics efficiency and regional prosperity.  
* **Green transition**—the focus on renewable power, waste‑to‑wealth and circular water economy align with UN SDGs 7, 11, 12 and 13, showcasing a scalable template for other metros.  
* **Decentralised self‑reliance**—by empowering elected local bodies, the plan decentralises decision‑making while maintaining a coherent vision for “Viksit Gujarat” and “Viksit Bharat.”  
* **Public‑private partnership (PPP) potential**—large capital investment (₹18,800 crore) invites stratified PPP models, promoting skilled‑labor creation and technology transfer.  

**4. UPSC RELEVANCE**  
* **Governance & Federalism** – Examining the PM’s role in catalysing state‑level implementation reflects central‑state coordination, a recurrent UPSC topic.  
* **Infrastructure & Development Planning** – The projects fall under the Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs, Ministry of Commerce & Industry, and Ministry of Power; analyzing them aids understanding of the National Infrastructure Pipeline.  
* **Sustainable Development** – The Surat case is a textbook example of balancing economic growth with environmental stewardship, crucial for Paper‑II (Environment & Ecology) and Paper‑III (Public Administration).  
* **Energy Policy & Self‑Reliance** – Gujarat’s renewable energy output, green‑steel initiative, and the emphasis on self‑reliance echo the themes of Energy Security & National Defence.  
* **Urban Planning & Public Health** – The ESIC hospital and sanitation projects illuminate Urban Development and Health & Family Welfare’s interlinkages.  

In sum, the Surat initiative is a consolidated model of modern, green, inclusive infrastructure, eminently apt for analysis under UPSC’s multidisciplinary framework, offering clear case‑study material for essays, essays‑on‑administration and subjective question elaborations.

headline: Union Minister Shri Jayant Chaudhary Inaugurates Daycare Facility for Children of MSDE Employees at Kaushal Bhawan, Reinforcing the Government’s Vision of an Inclusive Workplace
fullText:
Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship

Union Minister Shri Jayant Chaudhary Inaugurates Daycare Facility for Children of MSDE Employees at Kaushal Bhawan, Reinforcing the Government’s Vision of an Inclusive Workplace

In a significant step towards fostering a more inclusive, compassionate, and people-centric work environment, Shri Jayant Chaudhary, Minister of State (Independent Charge) for Skill Development and Entrepreneurship and Minister of State for Education, Government of India, inaugurated a new Daycare Facility for Children of employees at Kaushal Bhawan- the headquarters of the Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE).

Spread across nearly 600 sq. metres, the new Daycare Facility offers a welcoming, safe, and nurturing environment where children can learn, play, rest, and grow. The facility features comfortable sleeping and resting areas, an engaging play and activity zone, age-appropriate learning resources, child-friendly furniture, hygienic washrooms, and specially designed feeding and baby-changing rooms to ensure the comfort and well-being of infants and young children. Staffed by trained caregivers and support personnel, the crèche has been thoughtfully designed to cater to the developmental and care needs of children aged 6 months to 6 years.

The newly inaugurated Daycare facility goes beyond infrastructure—it embodies a culture that values care, celebrates childhood, and enables employees to balance professional commitments with family responsibilities. By bringing quality childcare closer to the workplace, the initiative aims to support working parents and reflects MSDE’s commitment to fostering a more inclusive, family-friendly, and people-centric work environment.

Smt. Debashree Mukherjee, Secretary, MSDE; Shri Dilip Kumar, Director General (Training), Directorate General of Training (DGT); Prof. (Dr.) Ashok Kumar Gaba, Executive Member, NCVET; Shri Niranjan Kumar Sudhansu, Additional Secretary, MSDE; Smt. Manisha Sensarma, Senior Economic Advisor, MSDE; Smt. Hena Usman, Joint Secretary, MSDE; Smt. Archana Mayaram, Economic Advisor, MSDE; Smt. Manasi Sahay Thakur, Joint Secretary, MSDE; Shri M. Subramanian, Joint Secretary, MSDE along with other senior officials from the ministry, DGT, NCVET, and NSDC, were present on the occasion. As India advances towards becoming a future-ready and inclusive economy, initiatives such as these reinforce the government’s focus on building institutions that are progressive, people-centric, and responsive to the needs of a modern workforce.
summaryText: The Ministry of Skill Development and Entrepreneurship (MSDE) plays a pivotal role in addressing workforce gaps by fostering inclusive economic growth. Central to its mandate is its recent inauguration of a childcare facility at Kaushal Bhawan, near offices, offering safe spaces for infants and toddlers. Key features include trained caregivers, age-appropriate resources, and parent-friendly design, emphasizing accessibility and holistic child development. This initiative supports employees balancing professional and familial responsibilities, enhancing recruitment and retention. Its significance lies in aligning with national goals for human resource empowerment and inclusive workplaces. From a UPSC perspective, such measures reflect policy focus on skill-building and sustainable development, connecting MSDE’s programs to broader developmental objectives. By integrating childcare infrastructure into public employment strategies, it reinforces strategic alignment with economic policy, highlighting MSDE’s contribution to skill-centric governance while promoting societal well-being. Such efforts underscore the Union’s commitment to nurturing a supportive ecosystem where employees and families thrive, making MSDE’s initiative both a practical milestone and a signaling moment for nationwide alignment with progress-driven agendas. headline: Reforms to Expand Foreign Participation in G-Secs
fullText:
Reforms to Expand Foreign Participation in G-Secs

Strengthening Bond Markets, Attracting Foreign Investment

The Government is undertaking sustained reforms to strengthen India's standing as a leading destination for global investment. To deepen the capital market, it has introduced a series of reforms to increase Foreign Portfolio Investor (FPI) participation in Government Securities (G-Secs). Key measures include tax exemptions on interest income, long-term capital gains (LTCG) and short-term capital gains (STCG), expansion of specified securities under the Fully Accessible Route (FAR), and streamlined investment norms.

These reforms aim to attract stable long-term foreign capital, deepen the G-Sec market, and strengthen India's debt market by broadening and diversifying the investor base. Greater foreign participation will provide an additional source of funding for infrastructure, manufacturing, urban development, climate initiatives, and other national priorities. It will also improve market liquidity and price discovery, support the development of a smoother yield curve, reduce government borrowing costs, strengthen financial market benchmarks, and enhance the transmission of monetary policy across the economy.

The reforms are poised to attract long-term institutional investors such as pension funds, insurance companies, and sovereign wealth funds, leading to more stable and sustained capital inflows. They are also expected to boost foreign exchange inflows and strengthen the resilience of India's financial markets.

Decoding FIIs, FPIs, G-Secs, BIS

Foreign Institutional Investment (FII) is a category of FPI that refers specifically to investments made by foreign institutional investors such as mutual funds, pension funds, insurance companies, and hedge funds. These institutions invest pooled funds in financial markets and typically play a more active role in investment research and decision-making.

Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI) refers to investments made by foreign individuals, institutional investors, or funds in financial instruments such as stocks, bonds, mutual funds, and government securities. FPIs do not participate in the management or decision-making of the companies in which they invest and are generally considered passive investors.

Government Securities (G-Secs), are tradeable debt instruments issued by the central/ state governments to fund public projects, manage fiscal deficits, control market liquidity.

The Bank for International Settlements (BIS) is an international financial institution owned by central banks. It serves as a forum for monetary and financial cooperation among central banks globally. It also acts as a banker and asset manager for central banks and international organizations.

Prior to the latest reform, Foreign Institutional Investors (FIIs), including SEBI-registered Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs), were taxed under Section 210 of the Income-tax Act, 2025. Any income earned from investments in Government Securities (G-Secs) was subject to tax.

Interest income earned on G-Secs was taxed at 20% for FIIs/FPIs.

Short-term capital gains arising from the sale of G-Secs were taxed at 30%, depending on the nature of the transaction.

Long-term capital gains were taxed at 12.5%.

As a result, a portion of the returns earned by foreign investors from holding or trading G-Secs was payable as tax in India.

Recognising the importance of a competitive tax framework in attracting global capital, the Government has introduced a tax exemption for FPIs/FIIs investing in G-Secs.

Under the new regime, FPIs/FIIs will be exempt from:

Interest income earned from G-Secs; and

Capital gains arising from the sale, transfer, exchange or redemption of G-Secs.

The exemption will apply to income arising on or after 1 April 2026. The Income-tax (Amendment) Ordinance, 2026 inserted specific provisions granting this exemption to FIIs investing in G-Secs.

Classification of Capital Gains

Long-Term Capital Gains (LTCG) arise when a Government Security is held beyond the prescribed holding period.

Listed G-Secs: More than 12 months.

Unlisted G-Secs: More than 24 months.

Short-Term Capital Gains (STCG) arise when a Government Security is held for less than the prescribed holding period.

Listed G-Secs: Up to 12 months.

Unlisted G-Secs: Up to 24 months.

Foreign investors can invest in Indian G-Secs through routes such as the General Route and the Fully Accessible Route (FAR). General Route is the standard channel for foreign investors. It allows them to buy and sell permitted Indian G-secs, but comes with certain restrictions, such as caps on how much can be invested in a particular security, how long it must be held, and an overall investment limit. FAR is an open-access channel where foreign investors can invest in select G-Secs without restrictions that apply under the General Route.

As on 12 May 2026, FPIs held G-Secs worth ₹3,75,171 crore, accounting for 3.34% of the total outstanding G-Secs stock of ₹112.42 lakh crore. Notably, FAR accounted for the majority of these investments, with FPI holdings of ₹3.21 lakh crore, representing 6.74% of the ₹47.63 lakh crore outstanding stock eligible under FAR.

FPI Investments in Government Securities (as on 12 May 2026)

Recognising its strong investor interest, the Government has introduced further reforms to expand the scope of FAR and encourage greater foreign participation in the G-Sec market.

Expansion of the Fully Accessible Route (FAR)

The Government has now expanded the list of securities eligible under the FAR, broadening investment opportunities for foreign investors across a wider range of G-Secs.

The FAR framework will now include:

New issuances of 15-year Government Securities;

New issuances of 30-year Government Securities;

New issuances of 40-year Government Securities; and

Sovereign Green Bonds (SGrBs) issued in FAR-eligible tenors.

The expansion is expected to broaden investment opportunities across the maturity spectrum and encourage greater participation in long-duration sovereign debt instruments.

Relaxation of Investment Restrictions under the General Route

To facilitate greater FPI participation in G-Secs, the Government has removed:

Short-term investment limit;

Security-wise investment limit.

However, the overall investment limits remain unchanged at:

6% of the outstanding stock of Central Government Securities; and

2% of the outstanding stock of State Government Securities (SGSs).

Further, the existing 'General' and 'Long-Term' categories for FPI investments will be merged into a single investment limit for Government Securities and State Government Securities respectively.

Towards Deeper, More Globally Integrated Capital Markets

By simplifying market access and reducing operational complexities, these measures will provide a more seamless investment experience aligned with leading global markets. Over time, they are expected to support greater inclusion of Indian bonds in global indices, attract long-term foreign capital, expand participation in both debt and equity markets, and further integrate India's financial system with the global economy. These reforms will encourage wider participation from global investors seeking opportunities in one of the world's fastest-growing major economies.

summaryText:
### **UPSC Study Note: Govt. Reforms to Expand FPI Participation in G-Secs**  

#### **1. CONTEXT**  
The Indian government is prioritizing reforms to deepen capital markets and attract foreign institutional investors (FIIs/FPIs), positioning India as a global investment hub. Key drivers include:  
- The current contribution of FPIs to Government Securities (G-Secs) is marginal (₹3.75 lakh crore, 3.34% of total outstanding G-Secs), despite strong investor demand under the Fully Accessible Route (FAR).  
- Global demand for low-risk, high-liquidity sovereign debt is growing, with India seeking to align with benchmark economies like the U.S. and EU.  

#### **2. KEY FEATURES**  
**A. Tax Reforms**  
- **Exemption for FIIs/FPIs**: Interest income and capital gains (LTCG/STCG) from G-Secs investments are tax-free (effective 1 April 2026).  
  - Removes burden of 20% tax on interest, 30% on STCG, and 12.5% on LTCG.  
- **Streamlined Investment Norms**:  
  - Removal of short-term caps and security-specific investment limits.  
  - Merged investment limits: 6% of Central G-Secs and 2% of State G-Secs (SGSs).  

**B. Structural Reforms**  
- **Expanded FAR**:  
  - Now includes 15-, 30-, and 40-year G-Secs, plus Sovereign Green Bonds (SGrBs).  
  - Eliminates restrictions on holding periods and investment thresholds.  
- **Streamlined General Route**:  
  - Eliminates operational hurdles like maximum investment duration and caps.  

#### **3. SIGNIFICANCE**  
- **Market Depth**: Increased foreign participation enhances liquidity, price discovery, and a smoother yield curve.  
- **Cost of Borrowing**: Greater competition among lenders reduces government borrowing costs.  
- **Monetary Policy Transmission**: Strengthens benchmarking for interest rates, aiding effective policy execution.  
- **Climate & Infrastructure Finance**: SGrB inclusion aligns with global ESG trends, channeling funds into sustainable projects.  
- **Global Integration**: Attracts long-term capital from pension funds, insurance companies, and sovereign wealth funds, boosting India’s credit rating and global index inclusion prospects.  

#### **4. UPSC RELEVANCE**  
- **Economic Policies**: Demonstrates proactive measures to deepen debt markets and promote inclusive growth.  
- **Financial Inclusion**: FPI participation bridges capital gaps for sectors like renewable energy and infrastructure.  
- **Global Integration**: Prepares India for greater exposure in international markets and global financial benchmarks.  
- **Environmental Governance**: Sovereign Green Bonds signal alignment with SDGs and climate finance initiatives.  
- **Economic Resilience**: Reduces reliance on domestic capital, enhancing fiscal flexibility amid global volatility.  

**Performance Metrics**: Current FPI share in G-Secs (3.34%) and FAR’s ₹3.21 lakh crore dominance highlight untapped potential, necessitating sustained reforms.  

**Conclusion**: These reforms are pivotal for India’s ambition to become a $5 trillion economy, fostering stability, innovation, and global competitiveness in financial markets.

headline: President Appoints Additional Judges as Permanent Judges of Bombay High Court
fullText:
Ministry of Law and Justice

President Appoints Additional Judges as Permanent Judges of Bombay High Court

In exercise of the powers conferred by the Constitution of India, the President of India, after consultation with Chief Justice of India, is pleased to appoint the following Additional Judges as Permanent Judges of the Bombay High Court:

Name of the Additional Judge

Ms. Nivedita Prakash Mehta

Appointed as Judges of the Bombay High Court

Prafulla Surendrakumar Khubalkar

Joint Secretary to the Government of India
summaryText:
**1. CONTEXT**  
In 2024, the President of India, after consulting the Chief Justice of India, effected a constitutional appointment. Two qualifying law professionals were elevated from their status as Additional Judges to Permanent Judges of the Bombay High Court. These appointments were announced by the Ministry of Law and Justice in a press release emphasising adherence to the Constitution and the collegium process.

**2. KEY FEATURES**  
- **Constitutional Basis**: Article 124(2) grants the President the power to appoint judges of high courts, subject to consultation with the Chief Justice of India.  
- **Dual Appointments**:  
  1. *Ms. Nivedita Prakash Mehta* – promoted on the basis of years of judicial experience and meritorious performance.  
  2. *Prafulla Surendrakumar Khubalkar* – a Joint Secretary‑level senior civil servant with a legal background, whose elevation reflects the inter‑linkage between civil service and judiciary.  
- **Permanent Status**: Conversion from “Additional” to “Permanent” judges removes the temporary tenure and affirms confidence in judicial competence.

**3. SIGNIFICANCE**  
- **Judicial Consolidation**: Strengthens the bench of the Bombay High Court, crucial for handling the high case‑load in Maharashtra’s commercial hub.  
- **Legal‑Policy Interface**: The appointment of a former Joint Secretary underscores the need for judges with policy‑making experience, bridging administrative and judicial domains.  
- **Rule of Law**: Reinforces the constitutional principle of judicial independence by ensuring only vetted, merit‑based appointments become long‑term fixtures.

**4. UPSC RELEVANCE**  
Students preparing for the UPSC Civil Services Examination should note:  
- **Constitutional Provisions**: Articles 124, 217, 222, 233 & 234 governing appointments, tenure, and transfer of high‑court judges.  
- **Judicial System Analysis**: The role of Additional vs Permanent judges, their appointment procedures, and impact on case‑disposition efficiency.  
- **Inter‑governmental Functions**: The interplay between civil service (e.g., Joint Secretary) and judiciary, relevant for the *Legal and Judicial Services* sub‑section.  
- **Current Affairs**: Incorporating recent appointees provides illustrative examples for the *Current Affairs* part of the General Studies Paper‑1 and Paper‑2(Objective & Essay) where judicial appointments and reforms are recurring themes.

headline: India’s Health Transformation
fullText:
India’s Health Transformation

In past 12 years, the Government has significantly strengthened India’s health system. It has insured over 44 crore families and operationalised over 1.86 lakh primary care centres. Generic medicines are now available at rates 50–90% below market price through over 18,000 Jan Aushadhi Kendras. Over 47 crore telemedicine consultations have been delivered. Medical colleges in India have more than doubled. 12 new AIIMS are functional since 2014. Traditional medicine has been formally integrated into the public health system. Maternal and child mortality rates have dropped significantly since 2014. TB incidence has fallen and malaria mortality is down 78%. Prevalence of various other diseases is also declining. Taken together, these gains reflect India's steady progress towards improved public health and universal health coverage.

Towards Universal Health Coverage

India has massively transformed its public health infrastructure in the past 12 years. It is now more accessible, affordable, and quality-driven. The impact is deeply visible even at the grassroots levels.

National Statistical Office 2025 survey of over 1.39 lakh households showcases more Indians than ever before are seeking medical care. Those visiting government hospitals and clinics for outpatient care — consultation, diagnosis, tests, prescription — are not having to pay anything. About half of the hospitalised patients are paying below Rs. 1,100 for their care. The poorest of households are benefitting from low or no out-of-pocket healthcare costs at such facilities. These are signs that people have renewed trust in the public healthcare system.

This did not happen by chance. Increased government spending and systematic healthcare upgrades have driven India's progress towards universal health coverage. From insurance schemes to expansion of healthcare infrastructure, India has systematically rebuilt its public health foundations over past 12 years.

The National Health Mission, with its myriad disease control and care programmes, is making healthcare delivery more targeted. A new and robust digital health infrastructure, along with budding artificial intelligence integrations, is taking healthcare delivery to the next level. To meet rising demand, the government has more than doubled its capacity to train doctors and nurses the system needs. At the centre of these changes is the world's most ambitious universal health coverage programme – the Ayushman Bharat scheme, launched in 2018.

Ayushman Bharat: Universal Health Coverage for Every Citizen

Ayushman Bharat — meaning Long Life India — provides affordable, quality healthcare to people from all walks and all stages of life. It has emerged as a critical lifeline for socio-economically weaker sections and senior citizens above 70 years. The Ayushman Bharat architecture rests on four key pillars that collectively strengthen preventive, promotive, curative, and digital healthcare systems across the country.

Pillar 1: Public Health Insurance Through Ayushman Bharat – Pradhan Mantri Jan Aarogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY)

In continuation of the National Health Mission, the government introduced the National Health Policy (NHP), 2017. It outlines a transformative vision for India’s healthcare system. The policy recognised emerging healthcare challenges, including the rise of non-communicable diseases and escalating treatment costs. To tackle these issues, the government in 2018 launched Ayushman Bharat - Pradhan Mantri Jan Aarogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY). It has now emerged as the world’s largest publicly funded health assurance scheme.

AB-PMJAY provides free public health insurance up to Rs. 5 lakh per family per year to socio-economically deprived families. Such families make up about 40 per cent of the population, or about 12 crore families. The insurance saves these families from catastrophic healthcare bills.

In a landmark expansion of the scheme in October 2024, the government introduced the Ayushman Bharat Vay Vandana, extending the insurance coverage to all senior citizens above 70 years.

The insurance covers consultations, hospitalisations and specialised treatment for a wide range of conditions, including cancer and heart diseases.

Empanelled public and private hospitals offer this insurance scheme. About 40,000 claims are processed daily for more than 1,900 treatment packages.

AB-PMJAY is available across the country, and has become a cornerstone of healthcare equity for lower-income people and senior citizens:

44.14 crore Ayushman Cards were created

12.03 crore hospitalisations were covered

Treatment worth Rs. 1,80,435 crore was provided

36,218 hospitals were empanelled — 19,659 public and 16,559 private

1.20 crore senior citizens were enrolled under the Ayushman Bharat Vay Vandana scheme, who availed over 13.84 lakh treatments worth Rs. 3,000 crore (as on June 5, 2026)

One-stop hub for PM-JAY beneficiaries – Available on both Android and iOS in 19 regional languages. Beneficiaries can:

Locate empanelled hospitals

Soni Kahtoon, wife of a daily wage worker from Motihari, Bihar, suffered from a heart valve condition for years while her family struggled financially. Her husband borrowed Rs. 2.5 lakh for treatment at a private hospital in Bihar, which brought no relief. Under AB-PMJAY, she received free heart valve replacement surgery at a multi-specialty hospital in Lucknow. She recovered and returned home to her children. Ayushman Bharat saved Soni and her family from a lifetime of financial duress.

Pillar 2: Primary Care Through Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (AAM)

The government is scaling primary healthcare infrastructure for less specialised treatment of minor ailments and for public outreach health programmes. Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (AAMs) are being established to provide primary healthcare at every neighbourhood. These AAMs are the cornerstone of community health in India, expanding universal health coverage. They provide comprehensive care spanning preventive, promotive, curative, rehabilitative and palliative care.

Each AAM offers 12 free services, going well beyond maternal and child healthcare. These include the screening and management of diseases, oral, eye and ear-nose-throat care, and mental health support. Teleconsultations, first-level emergency and trauma care, and free essential medicines and diagnostics are also provided at these centres. The AAMs have recorded a cumulative footfall of over 540 crores as on date. The government is rapidly building this infrastructure. There are now:

1.86+ lakh Ayushman Arogya Mandirs are functional, including: 1.34 lakh Sub Health Centres 24,483 Primary Health Centres 5,474 Urban Primary Health Centres 12,259 AYUSH centres 9,758 Urban Health and Wellness Centres (as of June 5, 2026)

1.34 lakh Sub Health Centres

24,483 Primary Health Centres

5,474 Urban Primary Health Centres

9,758 Urban Health and Wellness Centres (as of June 5, 2026)

AAMs are also centres for community health outreach for Community Health Workers and Accredited Social Health Activists (ASHAs). At these centres, they spread awareness of health programmes, check in on patients, and keep track of community health.

Improving Public Health at the Grassroot Level

For years, the Lalmati sub-centre in Assam's Bongaigaon district served over 10,000 people across 10 villages, providing only basic maternal and child health services. When the centre was upgraded to an AAM through the Ayushman Bharat scheme, it was truly transformed. The AAM expanded its services, adding screening of non-communicable diseases, mental health support, oral and eye care, and emergency services. Trained Community Health Officers were deployed to lead the team delivering these services.

Due to these changes, the area has recorded zero maternal and child mortality from preventable causes since 2024. High-risk pregnancies have more than halved. Cases of communicable diseases and anaemia have also declined.

Pillar 3: Pandemic Preparedness Through PM-ABHIM

Primary healthcare centres also play a pivotal role during public health emergencies, as seen during COVID-19. They become centres for disease surveillance, screening and treatment. The government has been significantly expanding the primary healthcare infrastructure to ensure pandemic preparedness.

Launched on October 25, 2021, the Pradhan Mantri Ayushman Bharat Health Infrastructure Mission (PM-ABHIM) is one of India’s largest pan-national programmes aimed at developing a resilient, accessible, and self-reliant public health system. Its total financial outlay is Rs. 64,180 crores, spanning from financial years 2021–22 to 2025–26.

The mission is building capacity at every tier of the health system and is:

Providing support for 23,224 rural health and wellness centres in 10 high-focus states.

Establishing: 13,736 urban health and wellness centres in all states. 3,389 block public health units in 11 high-focus states.

13,736 urban health and wellness centres in all states.

3,389 block public health units in 11 high-focus states.

Setting up 744 integrated public health labs in all districts

Establishing 631 critical care hospital blocks in all districts with a population of more than 5 lakh.

Beyond physical infrastructure, PM-ABHIM is also building India's pandemic defence architecture. This includes:

Dedicated investments in bio-security preparedness, pandemic research, and disease surveillance

Outbreak response and preparedness at 50 points of entry into the country

Critical Care Blocks with 150 beds being established across 12 central institutions

Pillar 4: Digital Health Ecosystem Through Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM)

A robust digital health infrastructure is as crucial for improving healthcare accessibility as brick-and-mortar infrastructure. At its core, it gives citizens ownership of their own health data — making it portable, accessible, and usable across any provider in the country. It creates a single source of truth for health facilities, professionals, and pharmacies, enabling better clinical decisions and continuity of care. It also creates a national database that the government can use to analyse public health trends, support medical research, and take a more targeted approach to interventions.

The fourth pillar, the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM), is focused on building a comprehensive, citizen-centric digital infrastructure. It was launched in September 2021. At the heart of ABDM is the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) — a unique 14-digit health identity number to which a citizen's complete health records are linked. With the citizen's consent, these records can be accessed by any healthcare professional across the ABDM network, enabling paperless, seamless care. ABDM also facilitates evidence-based decision-making for more effective health interventions at a population level.

The ABHA app, run by the National Health Authority, has a QR-based appointment registration service, which helps with long queues at hospitals or clinics. It also helps verify patient data. The app is leading the expansion of ABDM:

20.49 crore registrations were recorded on the app

27,328 facilities spanning 36 states and union territories were connected to the platform (as of March 31, 2026)

National Health Mission: Targeting Myriad Ailments and Diseases

The NHM with its two sub-missions — National Rural Health Mission and National Urban Health Mission — delivers targeted programmes that address specific diseases, populations, and health challenges.

NHM has improved public health outcomes across maternal and child health, disease elimination and immunisation.

Central to the NHM are community health workers operating from the primary healthcare centres. Due to the rapid expansion and upgradation of the primary healthcare infrastructure through Ayushman Bharat, these health workers are more capable than ever. They deliver healthcare services to more people, in more places, with better tools and data than before. Their work spans several targeted programme areas — beginning with maternal and child healthcare.

Maternal and Child Healthcare under National Health Mission

Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA): Launched in 2016, this scheme provides free and quality antenatal check-ups at government health facilities. Crores of women have benefitted from this scheme:

Over 7.47 crore pregnant women were examined

22,349 facilities provided PMSMA services nationwide (as of June 5, 2026).

Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY): This scheme supports women through childbirth. It encourages facility-based deliveries among poor pregnant women, particularly those from below-poverty-line households, and the scheduled caste and scheduled tribe communities.

Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK): This scheme entitles every pregnant woman at a public facility to government-funded free delivery, drugs, diagnostics, diet, and transport. There is no out-of-pocket cost. In 2014, the JSSK was extended to all antenatal & post-natal complications of pregnancy.

These programmes together have significantly reduced maternal mortality rates.

Community Health Workers Ensure Safe Institutional Births

The pregnancy of Mubashira (of Malappuram, Kerala) was identified as high-risk at Panakkad Urban Family Health Centre. Thereafter, ASHA workers conducted monthly home visits throughout her pregnancy. They monitored both maternal and foetal health. They provided antenatal care and vaccined Mubashira (tetanus and adult diphtheria vaccination). The local police station was also informed at the outset. Regular phone conversations helped build trust with the family. Due to the community outreach, Mubashira safely delivered the baby girl at a hospital.

The NHM runs several other schemes to take care of mothers and children. These include:

Surakshit Matritva Aashwasan: Guarantees free, dignified healthcare to every woman and newborn at public health facilities.

Midwifery Initiative: Trains Nurse Practitioner Midwives

Stillbirth Surveillance and Response: Tracks and reduces stillbirths, targeting fewer than 10 stillbirths per 1,000 births by 2030.

Home Based Newborn Care: ASHA workers conduct six home visits after birth to monitor newborn health and enable early referral.

Home Based Care of Young Child: ASHA workers conduct five home visits for children aged 3–15 months to support nutrition and early childhood development.

Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram: Screens children for birth defects, developmental delays, deficiencies, and diseases

Nutrition and Adolescent Health

Anaemia Mukt Bharat: Provides iron and folic acid supplementation to pregnant women, children, and adolescents.

Weekly Iron Folic Acid Supplementation: Provides weekly Iron and Folic Acid tablets to adolescents to prevent deficiency.

Mothers' Absolute Affection: Promotes breastfeeding and complementary feeding through frontline health worker training.

Adolescent Friendly Health Centres: Provide counselling and clinical services to adolescents at the primary healthcare level.

Menstrual Hygiene Scheme: Ensures adolescent girls have access to information, sanitary products, and safe disposal mechanisms.

Vaccination of newborns and pregnant mothers is crucial to ensure their health and longevity. The government runs the Universal Immunisation Programme (UIP), which targets 2.67 crore newborns and 2.9 crore pregnant women annually — offering free vaccines against 12 diseases.

Despite this programme, many children and pregnant women are not or partially vaccinated. In 2014, the government launched Mission Indradhanush as a catch-up programme for such communities with low vaccination rates.

Through Mission Indradhanush:

5.46 crore children and 1.32 crore pregnant women that were not immunised before were vaccinated

Across routine immunisation, over 95 per cent of children aged 12–23 months received their vaccinations at a public health facility in 2023–24, according to the National Health Family Survey (2023-2024).

Together, these efforts have significantly improved vaccination coverage nationwide.

The share of zero-dose children — those who have received no vaccines at all — fell from 0.11 per cent of the population in 2023 to 0.06 per cent in 2024.

India's immunisation programmes have also contributed to the elimination of maternal and neonatal tetanus, as certified by the World Health Organisation in May 2015.

To strengthen timely vaccination of children and pregnant women, the Government launched the U-WIN (Universal Immunisation Web-enabled Network) platform for a pan-India rollout in 2024. U-WIN digitally records every vaccination event, tracks due doses, and ensures permanent, portable health records for beneficiaries. It reduced gaps caused by missed or untracked vaccinations.

11.87 crore children and 3.96 crore pregnant women are registered on the platform (as of March 2026).

The government also launched a three-month cervical cancer prevention campaign in February 2026. The campaign provided free human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine to girls aged 14 years across the country, which number about 1.15 crore.

Beyond protecting mothers, children and young girls, NHM's programmes have also driven significant progress in eliminating communicable diseases that have long burdened India’s public health system.

Eliminating Communicable Diseases

The government has reduced the prevalence of communicable diseases over the past 12 years. NHM's targeted programmes for disease elimination and treatment have delivered measurable outcomes across tuberculosis, malaria, and leprosy.

India has reduced tuberculosis (TB) incidence through the National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP). The goal of the programme is to eliminate tuberculosis. This programme is multifaceted and includes screening and treatment of TB through government facilities.

Due to the programme, TB cases and fatalities have declined faster than the global rate of decline. A strong community support network established through the programme also helped in combating TB.

Over 3.78 lakh Nik-shay Mitras — volunteers who provide TB patients with nutritional, vocational and diagnostic support — supported over 20 lakh patients.

These volunteers provided over 45 lakh food baskets (as of December 2025). This support helped patients stay committed to their treatment over the full course of care.

Pradhan Mantri TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan: Launched in September 2022, the Pradhan Mantri TB Mukt Bharat Abhiyaan is the community-led component of the National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP). It transformed TB elimination into a Jan Andolan — a people's movement.

Through awareness drives and community mobilisation, over 20 crore individuals have been screened for TB and more than 28 lakh patients diagnosed since December 7, 2024.

This large-scale screening helped identify previously undetected TB cases circulating silently within communities.

India, once among the highest malaria-burden countries globally, has significantly reduced cases and deaths since 2017 through sustained and targeted government interventions via the NHM.

In 2016, the government launched the National Framework for Malaria Elimination, which provides a roadmap for eliminating malaria by 2027. Building on this framework, the National Strategic Plan for Malaria Elimination (2023–2027) introduced enhanced surveillance and a "test, treat, and track" approach to case management. It also developed real-time data tracking. These interventions have contributed to a sustained decline in both malaria cases and deaths across India.

Other Communicable Diseases

NHM's targeted programmes have driven measurable progress even beyond TB and malaria — reducing transmission, cutting fatality rates, and pushing several diseases towards elimination.

HIV-AIDS: The mother-to-child transmission rate has declined by about 74.5% between 2010 and 2024. This has outpaced the global decline of around 56.5% in the same period.

Kala-Azar: 633 endemic blocks in 54 districts have shown less than one case per 10,000 population.

Japanese Encephalitis: Case Fatality Rate has reduced from 17.6% (2014) to 7.1% (2024)

Dengue: Case Fatality Rate has reduced to 0.13% in 2024.

143 of 348 endemic districts (41%) have stopped Mass Drug Administration and cleared Transmission Assessment Survey, up from 15% in 2014 (MDA is the administration of drugs to whole populations irrespective of disease status).

MDA coverage improved from 75% (2014) to 85% (2025) against total population.

Leprosy: Districts achieving elimination (<1 case per 10,000) went up from 542 (2014-15) to 638 (2024-25). The new case detection rate has dropped from 9.73 per 100,000 in 2014-15 to 7.0 per 100,000 in 2024-25.

Districts achieving elimination (<1 case per 10,000) went up from 542 (2014-15) to 638 (2024-25).

The new case detection rate has dropped from 9.73 per 100,000 in 2014-15 to 7.0 per 100,000 in 2024-25.

COVID-19 and Pandemic Response

India’s pandemic response was among the most proactive. The government started screenings at airports before the first domestic case was reported. A dedicated vaccine task force was also formed in April 2020. India was among the first, globally, to introduce the Rapid Antigen Tests.

On January 16, 2021, India launched the National COVID-19 Vaccination Programme, which became one of the world’s largest vaccination drives. Over 220 crore doses — including two indigenous vaccines — were administered free of charge at government schools, clinics, hospitals, and other venues. Certificates were digitised and delivered via the COWIN digital platform.

The government rapidly scaled up its infrastructure during the pandemic:

Testing labs grew from 14 to 3,400

ICU beds increased from 2,168 to 1.45 lakh

Oxygen-supported beds went up from 50,583 to 5.15 lakh

Before the pandemic, India did not produce any PPE kits domestically. During COVID-19, domestic PPE manufacturing scaled from zero to more than 5 lakh kits per day.

Over 1,563 Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) oxygen plants were sanctioned, and about 900 Oxygen Express trains transported over 36,840 tonnes of liquid medical oxygen during times of crisis.

Under Vaccine Maitri, India supplied vaccines to nearly 100 countries — including free vaccines to 48 countries. Under this humanitarian initiative, India provided about 300 million doses. India truly lived the ethos of Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam — “the world is one family” — by standing with the world in times of need.

Prevention and Treatment of Non-Communicable Diseases

Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) — heart disease, diabetes, cancer, stroke — account for 60% of all deaths in India. To address this growing burden, NHM's National Programme for Prevention and Control of NCDs (NP-NCD) has been strengthening infrastructure for early detection, diagnosis, and treatment.

Early Detection and Screening

Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (AAMs) function as the cornerstone for community screenings of NCDs, including cancer. The government also runs various clinics and care centres for cancer screenings, diagnosis and management of the diseases.
